title: Tipping in plates and inserts
subtitle: Mounting loose sheets, maps, and folded inserts into a bound book
excerpt: Tipping in attaches a loose sheet to the edge of a page or signature with a narrow line of adhesive along one edge. The term describes both single plates mounted into a bound text and folded inserts added to an existing book. The technique is the standard way to add photographs, illustrations, and documents without rebinding.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on plates and inserts. The standard method for tipping in plates is documented across late-Victorian bookbinding manuals. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: Attaching a loose sheet to the edge of an adjacent page or signature using a narrow strip of adhesive along one edge only. The opposite edge of the tipped sheet hangs free so the page can open flat.
A narrow strip of paper used as an intermediary between a tipped-in plate and the book page. The guard absorbs the adhesive strain and prevents the plate from pulling on the page as humidity changes.

content.text: adds a loose sheet to the body of a book by gluing a narrow strip along one edge only. It is the standard method for adding illustrations, maps, folded inserts, and photographs to an existing binding without cutting it apart. Because only the one edge is glued, the tipped sheet opens freely and does not pull on the book page as the paper moves with changes in humidity.

items.name: PVA, acid-free (slug: pva-acid-free)
items.description: Use straight from the bottle. A thin coat along a 3 mm strip.

items.name: Paste brush, small (slug: paste-brush-bookbinding)
items.description: Or a fine watercolour brush for applying PVA precisely along the narrow edge strip.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For pressing the tipped edge firmly and burnishing the bond.

items.name: Metal ruler, 15 cm (slug: metal-ruler-15cm)
items.description: For guiding the narrow glue strip.

items.name: Strips of newsprint or scrap paper
items.description: To mask the area that should not receive adhesive.

content.text: Lay the plate face down on the cutting mat. Place a narrow strip of scrap paper across the plate, 4 mm from the left edge. Hold the scrap paper in place and brush PVA over the exposed 4 mm strip. Remove the scrap paper. This gives a clean glue line without adhesive straying onto the plate face.

Position the plate immediately. Open the book to the target page. Align the plate so the glued edge sits exactly on the facing page edge. Press down and burnish with the bone folder through a clean piece of scrap paper. Close the book and place it under a light weight for 10 minutes.

For plates on heavy or coated paper, the adhesive bond at a single narrow strip may not hold long-term. In this case, use a guard: a strip of Japanese tissue or thin washi, 8 mm wide. Brush PVA onto one half of the guard, attach it to the back edge of the plate, and allow to dry. Then brush the second half of the guard with PVA and attach it to the page. The guard bridges the gap and distributes the bond over a wider surface.
Tipping in a folded map or insert

A folded map or letter-sheet is tipped in along one folded edge. The insert folds down to the same height as the book pages; the spine edge of the fold receives the adhesive strip. The fold allows the insert to open to a larger format than the book itself.

For maps that need to open very flat, score an additional fold 6 mm in from the tipped edge before gluing. When the map is opened, the extra fold creates a step that prevents the heavy map sheet from pulling at the glued joint.

items.symptom: Tipped-in plate buckles across its face when the adhesive dries
items.cause: Too much PVA or adhesive spread over more than a 4 mm strip
items.fix: Re-wet the glued edge carefully with a damp swab to soften the PVA; gently peel back and re-press with a narrow, accurate strip of fresh adhesive.

items.symptom: Plate pulls free after a few weeks
items.cause: Paper grain running against the opening direction, or adhesive too diluted
items.fix: Use full-strength acid-free PVA. If the grain issue persists, use a guard to bond the plate more securely.
